page.big-retailers.title: Big retailers vs. a default Shopify cart | Persistent Cart
page.big-retailers.desc: Big retailers tie the cart to your account, so it follows you across devices and browsers. A default Shopify cart lives in a browser cookie — you can match it.
page.big-retailers.h1: Why big-store carts follow you across devices — and a default Shopify cart doesn't.
page.big-retailers.lede: Amazon and Walmart save your cart to your account, so a signed-in shopper sees the same cart on any device or browser. A default Shopify store keeps the cart in a two-week browser cookie instead — so it can't follow a shopper who switches devices. A sync layer closes that gap.
page.big-retailers.s1.h: Why do Amazon and Walmart tie the cart to your account?
page.big-retailers.s1.b:
Because your account is the one thing that travels with you between devices. When the cart is attached to your identity instead of to one browser, the store can show it to you anywhere you sign in — phone, laptop, tablet, or a mobile app. Amazon's help center describes the same idea: a cart that's available from any signed-in browser or its mobile app. Walmart, likewise, names cross-device cart access among the reasons to create an account.

This is a deliberate architecture choice, not a perk. The big retailers know shoppers research on one screen and buy on another, so they treat the cart as account data that lives on their servers. eBay similarly keeps a single cart at the account level. The result is a shopper expectation: people assume a saved cart will be waiting wherever they log back in.
page.big-retailers.s2.h: Why doesn't a default Shopify cart do the same?
page.big-retailers.s2.b:
Because on a default Shopify store the cart lives in your browser, not on your account. Shopify's own cookie policy lists a cookie named "cart" that "contains information related to the user's cart," with a two-week duration. That cookie stays on the single device and browser where it was created, so the cart can persist for the same shopper in the same browser for about two weeks — but it has no way to appear on a second device.

This isn't a bug; it's how the default storefront is built, and Shopify's community guidance has long noted that a cross-device cart "would require custom code." New customer accounts didn't change it either: as of 2026, a signed-in shopper still won't find a same-account cart waiting on a different device unless the store adds that capability. So the gap is specific — across devices and browsers — and not the same as saying Shopify doesn't save carts at all.
page.big-retailers.s3.h: Can a Shopify store match the big retailers?
page.big-retailers.s3.b:
Yes. The difference isn't budget or scale — it's where the cart is stored. A sync layer does for your store what the big retailers build in-house: it saves a signed-in customer's cart to their account, so it's there across devices and browsers, and on return visits. Persistent Cart adds exactly that to any Shopify store.

It works only for signed-in customers, the same as Amazon or Walmart — a guest with no account has nothing to sync to. By default it merges the two carts silently when a shopper signs in, with no popup, email, or opt-in; an optional prompt lets shoppers keep or combine carts if you prefer. Installing is one click from the App Store plus flipping one switch in your theme editor — no theme code. The outcome is the cart behavior shoppers already expect from the biggest stores.
